title: The Operations Audit for Manufacturing Companies Under 200 Employees
subtitle: Map what's working, what's duct-taped, and what's bleeding time before you spend a dollar on new tools

sections.title: Order Intake and Job Routing
sections.description: Where most manufacturers lose time without realizing it. Audit how orders land, get logged, and reach the floor.

items.text: Consolidate order intake into one channel instead of email, phone, text, and walk-ins
items.tip: Pick the channel 80% of orders already flow through and route the rest into it for 30 days

items.text: Log every order in one system before any work begins
items.tip: Kill sticky notes and verbal jobs. If it isn't in the system, it doesn't run

items.text: Route jobs to the right department or machine using written rules, not tribal knowledge
items.tip: Document the routing logic your senior operator uses in their head

items.text: Count how many times a single order is manually re-entered across systems
items.tip: Shadow one order end-to-end and tally each copy-paste moment

sections.title: Production Tracking and Floor Visibility
sections.description: If you can't see the floor in real time, you're managing by gut feel. Past 50 employees, that breaks.

items.text: Install a live view showing jobs in progress, queued, and blocked
items.tip: A shared digital board or even a TV with a Google Sheet beats a clipboard

items.text: Have operators log status changes as they happen, not at end of shift
items.tip: Put a tablet or scanner at each station so logging takes under 10 seconds

items.text: Track downtime events with reason codes instead of vague gaps
items.tip: Start with 6 codes: breakdown, material, QC wait, changeover, staffing, other

items.text: Pull actual vs. estimated production time for any recent job in under 5 minutes
items.tip: If it takes three people and a phone call, your data lives in silos

sections.title: Data, Systems, and Handoffs
sections.description: The hidden tax of disconnected tools. Every handoff between systems is a place errors hide and hours disappear.

items.text: List every system that touches an order from quote to shipment
items.tip: Include spreadsheets and personal inboxes. Shadow IT counts

items.text: Identify which handoffs between systems are manual copy-paste
items.tip: Circle each one in red. These are your automation candidates

items.text: Confirm one source of truth exists for inventory counts
items.tip: If the floor, office, and shipping each have their own number, you have zero

items.text: Verify customer data is consistent across CRM, quoting, and shipping
items.tip: Pull 10 random customers and compare addresses across systems

sections.title: People, Process, and Knowledge
sections.description: The stuff that walks out the door when someone quits. Audit what only lives in your team's heads.

items.text: Document the 3 processes that would break if your most senior operator left tomorrow
items.tip: Ask them to narrate while they work. Record it. Transcribe later

items.text: Confirm every critical role has a written backup or cross-trained operator
items.tip: Single points of failure are single points of bankruptcy

items.text: Review whether SOPs reflect how work is actually done, not how it was done 5 years ago
items.tip: Walk the floor with the SOP in hand. Mark every deviation

items.text: Audit which recurring meetings could be replaced by a dashboard or status update
items.tip: Cancel one meeting for 2 weeks and see if anyone notices

completion_cta.headline: You have the map. Now fix the right thing first.
completion_cta.description: Pick the 3 highest-impact items you flagged as broken and tackle those before buying any new software. Audit first, fix second, automate third.
